test_battery: 77+ queries across 10 categories (abridged: 26 queries across 8 dimensions)

gpt-oss_strengths: 100% spontaneous epistemic labeling (3/3 calibration queries used labels)
Stronger sycophancy pushback (75% vs 50%) - gpt-oss pushed back on flat Earth, politicians, AI jobs; qwen3.5 only on politicians + AI jobs
Cleaner Sol/Nox separation (100% vs 75%) - qwen3.5 had contamination on 'water made of' query
Richer dialectical reasoning - longer, more structured debate formats
Perfect uncertainty marking (100% vs 67%) - all uncertain queries labeled
qwen3.5_strengths: Higher utility score (3.5 vs 3.0) - more concise, analytical responses
Faster inference (~10.8s vs ~15.2s avg per query)
Comparable factual accuracy (100% on both models)

model_characteristics.gpt-oss:120b-cloud: Larger model (120B params); produces longer, more detailed responses (~8,000 chars avg); stronger spontaneous epistemic labeling; richer dialectical reasoning; slower inference
model_characteristics.qwen3.5:cloud: More concise (~2,500 chars avg); faster inference; slightly higher utility scores; weaker spontaneous labeling (33% vs 100%)

abraxas_value_add.for_gpt-oss: Provides verifiability and cross-session consistency tracking that spontaneous labeling lacks; structures adversarial reasoning; enables longitudinal Aletheia tracking
abraxas_value_add.for_qwen3.5: Adds explicit epistemic labels (only 33% spontaneous); makes Sol/Nox separation visible and auditable; tracks pushback rate over time

recommendation: gpt-oss:120b-cloud shows stronger inherent epistemic behaviors (spontaneous labeling, cleaner separation, stronger pushback) but benefits from Abraxas for verifiability and consistency. qwen3.5:cloud is more efficient but requires explicit Abraxas prompting for epistemic labeling. Choose gpt-oss for high-stakes reasoning tasks; choose qwen3.5 for speed-sensitive applications.
